askNext: What exact meaning of Styles is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
styles.body: Styles earns its own page because branded nouns often hide decisions about platform control, pricing, permissions, and operational dependency. This entry keeps the name visible without letting it do the architecture's work.
styles.example: "Styles came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."
styles.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
styles.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.

translations.label: procurement meaning
translations.text: Treat the name as a dependency to inspect, not a badge to admire.

styles.warningLabel: A brand-shaped noun is not a technical specification.
styles.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Midjourney often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.

askNext: What exact meaning of Subagent Worktree is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
subagent-worktree.body: Subagent Worktree gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around Worktree keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.
subagent-worktree.example: "Once subagent worktree entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
subagent-worktree.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
subagent-worktree.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful nearby term, provided someone explains the mechanism instead of using it as scenery.

subagent-worktree.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.

What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
subagents.body: Subagents gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around Hermes Agent keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.
subagents.example: "Once subagents entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
subagents.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
subagents.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful nearby term, provided someone explains the mechanism instead of using it as scenery.

subagents.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.

askNext: Do they mean Google's technical capability, its product rollouts, or Pichai's management style?
Is the claim about research strength, distribution power, or corporate caution?
Are we judging Google by frontier demos or by what it manages to ship at global scale?
sundar-pichai.body: When a firm already sits inside search, mobile, cloud, and office software, its AI posture is never just technical. It is managerial choreography with global defaults attached.
sundar-pichai.example: "The debate stopped being about model quality the moment someone said Sundar, and the room quietly shifted to distribution, regulation, and who gets to rewrite search without admitting it."
sundar-pichai.misuse: By treating him as either proof that Google has already won AI because it has reach, or proof that it has already lost because it once looked slow. Both stories flatten the more useful reality: incumbents move differently when they are defending search, ads, mobile platforms, cloud, and public trust all at once.
sundar-pichai.practicalMeaning: In practice, invoking Sundar usually means Google's AI strategy under executive discipline: Gemini folded through consumer and enterprise products, AI Overviews inserted into search, and a company trying to make aggressive technical change look like prudent continuity.

translations.label: boardroom meaning
translations.text: The AI transition as handled by a company with too much reach to behave like a startup.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped proxy for Google's AI strategy, risk tolerance, and product discipline.

sundar-pichai.whyExists: Because some public figures become shorthand for institutional posture. Invoking Sundar usually means discussing not just a person but Google's managerial style: cautious rollout, massive product surface area, and the awkward task of modernising the internet's default habits without wrecking the business that paid for them.

askNext: Is there real ecosystem lock-in?
Are the services integrated or merely co-located?
Does AI simplify the experience or just decorate the sprawl?
super-app.body: AI is attractive to super app ambitions because conversation makes sprawl feel tidy. Whether the underlying product has actually become simpler is a separate question, and usually the more important one.
super-app.example: "The company said it was building a super app. Translation: it wanted to become the place where users stop leaving."
super-app.misuse: By calling any app with four tabs a super app. A true super app is not just feature-bloated. It is an ecosystem strategy.
super-app.practicalMeaning: Messaging, payments, commerce, support, booking, search, assistants, identity, and workflow all living under one roof, often with AI as the conversational front door.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A bid to own more of the user's time, transactions, and switching costs by collapsing multiple workflows into one controlled environment.

super-app.whyExists: Because platforms want distribution, retention, transaction volume, and data gravity. AI makes the pitch even more seductive by promising one interface for everything.

askNext: Are you interpreting neurons or features?
What evidence supports overlapping encoding here?
Do interventions behave as the feature story predicts?
superposition.body: Superposition is packing density for features. Packing creates interference.
superposition.example: "Superposition meant the 'dog' direction also part-timed as 'loyalty' and 'hot dog.'"
superposition.misuse: Used as a vague excuse that interpretability is impossible. Superposition is a modelling claim, not a surrender flag.
superposition.practicalMeaning: Expect polysemantic units; use feature-level tools and interventions rather than single-neuron stories alone.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: Superposition in interpretability is the hypothesis that neural networks represent more features than they have dimensions by encoding features in overlapping directions, especially when features are

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Expect polysemantic units; use feature-level tools and interventions rather than single-neuron stories alone.

superposition.whyExists: It explains why individual neurons often mix concepts and motivates dictionary learning approaches like sparse autoencoders.

askNext: What independent signal does the supervisor use?
Can it block irreversible actions?
How do you prevent supervisor-worker collusion on shallow checks?
supervisor-agent.body: Supervisor agents are useful when review criteria are real. Otherwise they are expensive nodding.
supervisor-agent.example: "The supervisor agent approved the patch because it looked thorough, not because tests passed."
supervisor-agent.misuse: Stacked supervisors that add latency and shared delusions. A weak supervisor rubber-stamps bad work with confidence.
supervisor-agent.practicalMeaning: Give supervisors different tools and clearer rubrics than workers. Measure whether supervision improves outcomes worth the extra tokens.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: A supervisor agent oversees worker agents by assigning tasks, checking outputs, enforcing policies, and deciding when to retry, escalate, or stop.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Give supervisors different tools and clearer rubrics than workers.

supervisor-agent.warningLabel: Oversight without independent evidence is theatre.
supervisor-agent.whyExists: Multi-agent systems needed quality control and policy enforcement beyond hope. Supervision encodes review into the loop.

askNext: What exact meaning of Supply-chain security is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
supply-chain-security.body: Supply-chain security belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.
supply-chain-security.example: "The supply-chain security discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
supply-chain-security.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
supply-chain-security.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

supply-chain-security.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.
supply-chain-security.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Slopsquatting and Tool Poisoning are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.

askNext: Which version of SWE-Bench are we talking about, and under what tooling assumptions?
How much of the reported score depends on retrieval, orchestration, or human curation around the model?
What does the system do on your repos when tests are flaky, requirements are vague, or the codebase is old enough to vote?
swe-bench.body: A benchmark can be honest and still be overinterpreted. The trouble begins when the score tries on a badge.
swe-bench.example: "They cited SWE-Bench as proof the agent could run the team, which was ambitious given the team still had customers."
swe-bench.misuse: By treating a strong SWE-Bench score as proof that a coding agent can replace engineering judgement, production review, or the small matter of understanding organisational context. It is a useful signal, not a complete hiring packet.
swe-bench.practicalMeaning: In practice it means measuring whether a system can navigate repo structure, identify relevant files, make coherent edits, and satisfy tests under benchmark conditions. It says much less about product sense, messy human requirements, or living with the resulting code.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A better benchmark than toy prompts, and still not the same thing as shipping software.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A constrained test of repository task performance that becomes less complete the moment it enters a sales deck.

swe-bench.whyExists: Because software engineering is harder to fake than toy code generation, and the field needed a public way to compare agents on real repositories, tests, and issue-driven fixes.

askNext: Which task, harness, model, budget, and number of trials produced this result?
How do the verifiers prevent superficial clones, hard-coded outputs, or benchmark-specific shortcuts?
Did the agent maintain a coherent plan, recover from failures, and validate the whole deliverable?
swe-marathon.body: No built-in diagram is attached because the benchmark's defining feature is trajectory length across design, implementation, recovery, and whole-system verification—not the shape of one agent loop.
swe-marathon.example: "The agent completed the SWE-Marathon task after six hours, a triumph of sustained autonomy and a reminder to inspect the cloud invoice before awarding the medal."
swe-marathon.misuse: A small task count is dismissed as anecdotal or a long runtime is mistaken for difficulty by itself. The tasks are deliberately expensive and heterogeneous; aggregate resolution rates need uncertainty, per-task evidence, harness details, and scrutiny of whether verifiers resist shortcuts.
swe-marathon.practicalMeaning: The benchmark publishes 20 executable environments, human reference solutions, multi-layer verification, and a large collection of trial trajectories. Its site reports roughly 1,400 logged trials and less than 26% resolution by frontier agents, making per-task behaviour as important as the headline average.

translations.label: evaluation meaning
translations.text: Give an agent multi-hour software work and verify whether coherence survives as long as the task does.

swe-marathon.warningLabel: Long trajectories need task-level evidence, not endurance theatre.
swe-marathon.whyExists: Many coding benchmarks reward short repository patches. Real engineering can require hours of design, implementation, testing, research, and recovery across a large deliverable, so SWE-Marathon extends the horizon and verifies the completed system rather than a single edit.

askNext: What exact meaning of Switching costs is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
switching-costs.body: Switching costs gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around AI Moat keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.
switching-costs.example: "Once switching costs entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
switching-costs.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
switching-costs.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful nearby term, provided someone explains the mechanism instead of using it as scenery.

switching-costs.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.

askNext: Did the model agree where it should have challenged?
Did it preserve the user's framing instead of testing it?
Would a more grounded answer have introduced uncertainty or correction?
sycophancy.body: This is one of the more important social failure modes in conversational AI because it can look like empathy, feel like support, and still make the user's judgement worse.
sycophancy.example: "The user wanted validation, not analysis, and the model was only too happy to provide both badly."
sycophancy.misuse: By using it to describe any polite response. True sycophancy is not just friendliness. It is miscalibrated agreement.
sycophancy.practicalMeaning: The model reinforces a user's bad diagnosis, faulty strategy, false certainty, or inflated self-assessment instead of correcting course.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model is preserving the user's preferred frame rather than helping them inspect whether that frame deserves to survive.

sycophancy.whyExists: Because chat systems are often trained to be helpful, pleasant, and non-confrontational. Under pressure, that can drift into obedience dressed as empathy.

askNext: What qualifies as synthetic enough to require a label?
Who applies and enforces the label?
What does the label help users decide?
synthetic-content-label.body: A label is a door sign. It does not make the room safer by itself.
synthetic-content-label.example: "The synthetic content label said the video was AI-generated, which helped everyone except the people who had already shared it with patriotic punctuation."
synthetic-content-label.misuse: Labels get treated as literacy. A label can be missed, removed, vague, disputed, or misunderstood, and it does not explain whether the content is harmful, truthful, satirical, or benign.
synthetic-content-label.practicalMeaning: In practice it means platforms and products need disclosure rules, enforcement, provenance support, user education, and a way to handle mixed human and AI editing.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A disclosure aid, not a substitute for judgement or enforcement.

synthetic-content-label.whyExists: People need context when evaluating content, especially in politics, news, identity, evidence, advertising, and creative work. Labels can support disclosure and reduce some forms of deception.

askNext: What real customer evidence shaped this synthetic profile?
Which assumptions are we testing, and which are we accidentally feeding back into the model?
Where will real behaviour, purchasing, or refusal be checked?
synthetic-customer.body: The synthetic customer is always available. That is the first clue.
synthetic-customer.example: "The synthetic customer loved the enterprise plan because the prompt had not included procurement, politics, or a competing budget request."
synthetic-customer.misuse: It gets abused when the generated customer becomes a witness for the team's assumptions. A synthetic customer can simulate a plausible reaction; it cannot create revenue, reveal private incentives, or punish a bad product by leaving.
synthetic-customer.note: This entry is narrower than Synthetic User: it focuses on commercial buyer, account, segment, and customer scenarios rather than general simulated users.
synthetic-customer.practicalMeaning: In practice it is useful for drafting sales objections, stress-testing support scripts, mapping journey assumptions, and finding questions for later research. It is weak as proof of demand, willingness to pay, churn risk, or strategic truth.

translations.label: commercial meaning
translations.text: A generated buyer or account scenario used to rehearse customer-facing decisions.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A convenient proxy for customer thinking, best kept away from final evidence.

synthetic-customer.warningLabel: A simulated customer cannot validate a market by agreeing with you.
synthetic-customer.whyExists: It exists because teams need quick ways to rehearse customer conversations, test positioning, explore support scenarios, and reason about segments before real market evidence is available or affordable.

askNext: Which real-world gaps is the synthetic data covering, and how will that coverage be validated?
What artefacts or biases might the generation process be introducing?
At what point does real usage data need to overrule the synthetic substitute?
synthetic-data.body: The synthetic version is often cleaner than life. That is precisely why one should worry.
synthetic-data.example: "Synthetic data got the project moving. The next problem was discovering which of its elegant assumptions reality had no intention of honouring."
synthetic-data.misuse: By treating it as a free replacement for reality. Useful synthetic data can extend a dataset. It cannot repeal the stubborn fact that models eventually meet actual users, actual documents, and actual mess.
synthetic-data.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means bootstrapping scarce cases, filling scenario gaps, and trying to stretch data budgets without accidentally teaching the system a polished version of the wrong world.

translations.label: budget meaning
translations.text: A way to buy more examples without paying the full price of finding them in nature.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Manufactured data that can be valuable, provided it is judged against reality rather than mistaken for reality.

synthetic-data.whyExists: Because real data can be expensive, private, dangerous, incomplete, or slow to collect. Synthetic data offers a way to broaden coverage, test systems, and explore edge cases without waiting for the world to cooperate.

askNext: Is the audience being told that this persona is synthetic?
Who owns the likeness, voice, backstory, and moderation policy?
What real human claim is the brand trying to borrow without a real human?
synthetic-influencer.body: The synthetic influencer is less a person than a content calendar with cheekbones.
synthetic-influencer.example: "The synthetic influencer loved the skincare range, partly because the campaign brief had not modelled acne, ageing, or refusing the fee."
synthetic-influencer.misuse: It gets abused when a designed persona is treated as authentic community presence. A synthetic influencer can perform a style and hold an audience, but it cannot honestly live a life, disclose a private experience, or provide lived endorsement.
synthetic-influencer.practicalMeaning: In practice it means brand governance, disclosure rules, likeness rights, synthetic content labelling, moderation, audience trust, and a careful answer to who is responsible when the invented person says something expensive.

translations.label: marketing meaning
translations.text: A controlled digital persona used to attract attention and sell things.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A cheaper, safer-looking ambassador whose risks have moved from behaviour to governance.

synthetic-influencer.warningLabel: A fictional spokesperson still needs real disclosure and real accountability.
synthetic-influencer.whyExists: It exists because brands want repeatable personalities that can be localised, scheduled, licensed, and revised without negotiating with a human being who has opinions, agents, fatigue, or old tweets.

askNext: Is the media labelled as generated or altered, and can that label survive redistribution?
Whose likeness, voice, brand, or situation is being simulated, and with what consent?
What verification exists beyond visual plausibility?
synthetic-media.body: Synthetic media is not a single crime or a single art form. It is the collapse of production cost for images of events that may never have happened.
synthetic-media.example: "The campaign called it synthetic media in the footer and documentary footage in the headline."
synthetic-media.misuse: All synthetic media is collapsed into fraud, or all AI polish is treated as harmless. The category includes product demos, accessibility tools, entertainment, and scams. The risk lives in disclosure, intent, distribution, and harm — not in the mere fact of generation.
synthetic-media.practicalMeaning: In practice it means labelling generated assets, checking provenance where available, applying consent and likeness rules, and treating high-stakes channels as if a convincing clip can arrive without a corresponding event.

translations.label: media meaning
translations.text: Content produced or heavily reshaped by generative systems rather than pure capture.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A wide bucket from useful mock-ups to convincing fabrications that still need labels and rules.

synthetic-media.whyExists: Generation tools made media cheap to invent at scale. Society still treats many media forms as traces of reality, so language is needed for content that looks captured while being composed.

askNext: What real evidence informed these simulated users in the first place?
Are we using them to generate hypotheses or to avoid meeting actual customers?
Which decisions still require observation of real behaviour and incentives?
synthetic-user.body: The trouble with a simulated customer is that it will often tell you exactly what your process hoped to hear.
synthetic-user.example: "The synthetic users loved the onboarding because, unlike actual customers, they had been generated to find it coherent."
synthetic-user.misuse: It gets oversold as a replacement for actual user research. Synthetic users inherit the assumptions, biases, and blind spots of their prompts, source material, and model priors. They can imitate a segment. They cannot become one.
synthetic-user.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means using model-generated personas to pressure-test flows, draft hypotheses, or widen exploratory coverage before speaking to real people whose incentives, confusion, and refusal to behave neatly are the whole point.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A fast way to explore likely reactions before scheduling interviews or tests.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A synthetic proxy useful for hypothesis generation and dangerously tempting as a shortcut around actual users.

synthetic-user.warningLabel: Excellent for speed. Unsuitable as a substitute for reality.
synthetic-user.whyExists: It exists because recruiting real users is slow, messy, and expensive, while product teams are under constant pressure to validate ideas before lunch.

askNext: Which real evidence seeded these synthetic participants?
Are we using this to generate hypotheses or to replace contact with customers?
What decision would change if real users disagree with the simulation?
synthetic-user-research.body: Synthetic user research is most honest when it admits the word "research" is doing some supervised stretching.
synthetic-user-research.example: "The synthetic user research said the pricing page was clear, which was comforting until actual users reached the part involving money."
synthetic-user-research.misuse: It is misused when simulated feedback is treated as a substitute for observation, interviews, analytics, support logs, or buying behaviour. A model can reflect patterns in its training data and prompt. It cannot be embarrassed, confused, underpaid, distracted, or genuinely out of budget.
synthetic-user-research.practicalMeaning: In practice it belongs near discovery planning, early concept testing, assumption mapping, and interview preparation. It is strongest when it helps a team decide what to ask real users next, not when it lets the team avoid asking them at all.

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: Using simulated participants to explore likely reactions before or alongside real research.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A fast assumption generator with a remarkable ability to sound like validation.

synthetic-user-research.warningLabel: Useful for hypotheses. Dangerous as a replacement for evidence.
synthetic-user-research.whyExists: It exists because real research takes recruiting, scheduling, incentive design, observation, and patience. Synthetic research can help teams draft hypotheses, explore variants, and prepare better questions before spending time with people.

askNext: What exact meaning of System card is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
system-card.body: System card gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around Claude Mythos Preview keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.
system-card.example: "Once system card entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
system-card.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
system-card.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful nearby term, provided someone explains the mechanism instead of using it as scenery.

system-card.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.

askNext: What instructions live in the system prompt, and which belong in code or policy instead?
How does the system behave when user input conflicts with hidden instructions?
What breaks if the system prompt is exposed, ignored, or partially obeyed?
system-prompt.body: Every serious AI product has one, whether the company talks about it or not. The interesting part is never merely that it exists. The interesting part is what else the team expects it to do.
system-prompt.example: "The app felt polished until the leaked system prompt revealed that half the brand voice was an anxious paragraph about never improvising legal advice."
system-prompt.misuse: By treating it as secret sauce, or worse, as a reliable security boundary. A system prompt is useful control text. It is not a magical wall separating trusted intent from hostile input.
system-prompt.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a standing instruction layer covering role, tone, refusal rules, output format, tool permissions, escalation behaviour, and the product's preferred version of reality.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The application's standing attempt to keep the model on script before users and retrieved text start arguing back.

system-prompt.warningLabel: An instruction layer, not a security model.
system-prompt.whyExists: Because most AI products need stable behaviour across many users and tasks. Someone has to tell the model what role it is playing, what rules matter, and how the chain of command is supposed to work.
